synopsis: This practical guide teaches deep learning through a top-down approach, showing Python programmers how to train state-of-the-art models using the fastai library and PyTorch with minimal mathematics. It covers computer vision, natural language processing, tabular data, and collaborative filtering, progressively revealing the theory behind each technique.

sources.title: Deep Learning for Coders with fastai and PyTorch - O'Reilly

synopsis: Written by Christopher M. Bishop and Hugh Bishop, this Springer textbook presents the core concepts and contemporary architectures of deep learning with clarity, using textual descriptions, diagrams, mathematical formulae, and pseudo-code throughout. It targets both newcomers and experienced practitioners, emphasizing ideas likely to endure beyond current rapid advances in the field.

sources.title: Deep Learning: Foundations and Concepts - Springer

synopsis: This MIT Press textbook covers the full range of deep learning, from the mathematical foundations—linear algebra, probability, and optimization—to advanced topics such as convolutional networks, sequence modeling, autoencoders, and deep generative models. It addresses both the practical techniques used in industry and the theoretical research perspectives that underpin modern AI.

This Manning Publications guide teaches software engineers to build, train, and deploy neural networks using PyTorch by working through realistic projects, including a cancer-detection system built from medical imaging data. It covers diagnosing training problems, applying data augmentation, refining model architecture, and fine-tuning to improve real-world results.

sources.title: Deep Learning with PyTorch - Manning Publications

synopsis: Eric Topol surveys how artificial intelligence can transform medicine by handling administrative burdens, interpreting medical images, and supporting diagnosis, thereby freeing physicians to spend more time with patients. The book contrasts this vision of deep, human-centered care with the distracted, time-pressured reality of contemporary clinical practice.

Nick Bostrom examines what life could look like after technology and superintelligent AI have solved humanity's major problems—disease, poverty, labor—yet also rendered traditional human purpose uncertain. Through interleaved fiction and philosophical lectures, the book explores what values, meaning, and flourishing might look like in a post-instrumental world.

Gregory Makoff recounts Argentina's 2001 sovereign default on $100 billion in bonds and the fifteen-year legal conflict that reshaped sovereign debt law through U.S. court rulings affecting international creditors. The book traces IMF involvement, Argentina's debt restructuring negotiations, and the 2016 settlement that finally resolved most claims.

sources.title: Default - Center for Global Development Book Launch

synopsis: Felix Denk and Sven von Thülen compile an oral history of Berlin's techno scene in the years following the fall of the Wall, drawing on 150 interviews with DJs, club owners, artists, and promoters. The book documents how vacant buildings in reunified Berlin became the incubators of a global electronic music culture centered on clubs like Tresor and E-Werk.

sources.title: Der Klang der Familie - Google Books

synopsis: Martin Kleppmann examines the principles and trade-offs behind the databases, message queues, and distributed systems that power modern data-intensive applications, covering consistency, replication, partitioning, and stream processing. The book equips software engineers and architects to make informed decisions when choosing and combining data technologies for reliable, scalable systems.

Chip Huyen presents a holistic framework for building machine learning systems that work reliably in production, covering training data creation, feature engineering, model selection, deployment, monitoring, and continual learning. Each design decision is evaluated in terms of its effect on the system's overall goals rather than as an isolated technical choice.

Graham Allison applies the concept of Thucydides's Trap—the structural stress that arises when a rising power threatens to displace a ruling one—to the rivalry between China and the United States, surveying sixteen historical cases over five centuries. The book argues that war is the most common outcome of such transitions and explores the conditions under which it might be avoided.

sources.title: Destined for War - Houghton Mifflin Harcourt

synopsis: Robert Sapolsky draws on neuroscience, genetics, chaos theory, and psychology to argue that human behavior is fully determined by biological and environmental factors, leaving no room for free will in any meaningful sense. The book then considers the consequences of this conclusion for moral responsibility, punishment, and the design of more humane social institutions.

Through a series of interviews conducted from 2007 to 2009, an anonymous hedge fund manager explains the mechanics of the global financial crisis in real time to the editors of the literary magazine n+1. The conversations cover the collapses of Bear Stearns and Lehman Brothers, the dollar's decline, government bailouts, and the Madoff scandal as events unfold.

sources.title: Diary of a Very Bad Year - n+1 Shop

synopsis: Set in the thirtieth century, this hard science fiction novel follows Yatima, a digital orphan born in a virtual polis, as cosmic catastrophe forces a diaspora of uploaded minds across the galaxy in search of answers to an existential threat. Greg Egan uses the premise to explore questions of consciousness, identity, and the nature of existence in a civilization that has moved beyond biological bodies.

Nathaniel Popper traces the history of Bitcoin from its anonymous creation in 2009 through 2014, profiling the developers, entrepreneurs, and idealists who built the first decentralized digital currency and fought to establish it as a legitimate financial system. The book chronicles both the technical innovations and the tumultuous community conflicts that shaped Bitcoin's early years.

Thomas Sowell challenges the assumption that discrimination is the primary explanation for economic disparities among groups, presenting empirical evidence that outcomes depend on multiple factors including geography, culture, and the timing of demographic shifts. The enlarged edition extends his analysis to the social consequences of policies built on that single-cause assumption.

sources.title: Discrimination and Disparities - Hachette Book Group

synopsis: George Roberts, co-founder and longtime president of Teledyne, narrates how Henry Singleton built the company from a small aerospace firm into a diversified $4 billion conglomerate through aggressive acquisitions, financial discipline, and a controversial stock-buyback strategy. The memoir draws on Roberts's personal notes, board records, and shareholder reports to document Teledyne's rise, controversies, and eventual merger with Allegheny Steel.

Aston Zhang, Zachary Lipton, Mu Li, and Alexander Smola present deep learning through interactive Jupyter notebooks that integrate mathematical exposition, diagrams, and runnable code supporting PyTorch, TensorFlow, and MXNet. The book covers both foundational concepts and advanced architectures, and has been adopted as a course text at hundreds of universities worldwide.

sources.title: Dive into Deep Learning - Official Site

synopsis: William Janeway, drawing on decades as a venture capitalist, argues that the innovation economy is shaped by a three-player game among markets, the state, and financial speculators, with each player performing a distinct and necessary function. The book traces this dynamic from the First Industrial Revolution to the digital age, explaining why government investment and speculative bubbles have both been indispensable drivers of transformative technology.

sources.title: Doing Capitalism in the Innovation Economy - Cambridge University Press
